nativeRange.habitat: Adults tend to occur in groups along ledges on steep reef drop-offs, generally at depths greater than 20 m, while juveniles have been observed at around 50 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented hunting small crustaceans, worms, small bivalves, and invertebrates. Tends to accept a range of frozen meaty foods including mysis shrimp, krill, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and chopped seafood. Quality marine flakes and pellets have also been accepted. Multiple small feedings daily are generally recommended.

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Marinehome. 600x445. Sharp, vibrant live individual clearly showing the diagnostic white/pink body with red candystripes, black dorsal spot, and dark barred crescent (sepia) tail. Caveats: modest resolution and a small 'Marinehome' corner watermark; only license-passing correctly-ID'd photo found.

summary: A small, vividly striped hogfish from the deep reefs of the Western Central Pacific. Generally considered hardy and adaptable, though adults tend to grow more territorial and may prey on smaller ornamental shrimp.
careNotes: The Pacific Redstripe Hogfish is generally considered hardy and tends to adapt well to aquarium conditions. Juveniles tend to be peaceful, though adults have been known to bully smaller or more docile tankmates such as firefish, flasher wrasses, and seahorses. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised — this species has been documented jumping from open aquaria. Keeping only one individual per aquarium is generally recommended unless a very large system is available for groups of five or more introduced simultaneously. Like a number of wrasses, the Pacific Redstripe Hogfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females and the dominant specimen in a group may later transition to male. The species is generally considered reef safe with respect to corals and sessile invertebrates, but well-established adults have been reported to prey on smaller ornamental shrimp introduced after the fish. Ample live rock with caves and overhangs is advised both for foraging and refuge. In the wild this species occurs along deep steep-reef drop-offs at 20–75 m, so stable, well-oxygenated water with moderate to high circulation is generally recommended.
breedingNotes: The Pacific Redstripe Hogfish has been documented as egg-scattering (oviparous), with distinct male–female pairing observed during spawning. The species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females and the socially dominant fish in a group may transition to male over time. Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous, with distinct pairing observed during spawning in the wild. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite. No captive breeding has been documented in the aquarium hobby.
